html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谨按 - A formal expression used to introduce a scholarly or official statement.
  • 误也 - Indicates that there is an error in the attribution of the text to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钦定四库全书 - Refers to the *Siku Quanshu*, a massive compilation of Chinese texts commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
  • 子部十二 - Refers to the classification system within the *Siku Quanshu*, where texts are categorized into four major sections: Classics, History, Philosophy, and Miscellaneous.
  • 续博物志 - A continuation of an earlier work, likely *Wuzazu* (博物志), which was a compendium of various subjects.
  • 小説家三【琐记之属】 - Refers to the category of 'Miscellaneous Notes' within the 'Novel' section of the *Siku Quanshu*.
  • 提要 - A summary or preface, often providing background information on the text.
  • 旧本题晋李石撰 - Indicates that the original version of the text was attributed to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.
  • 今上于前朝作镇睢阳 - Refers to the current emperor (likely referring to the Song dynasty) who was previously stationed in Suizhou (睢阳).
  • 开国号大宋 - Refers to the founding of the Song dynasty.
  • 全摭陆佃埤雅之説 - Indicates that the text draws heavily on the work of Lu Dian's *Piyaya*.
  • 引子华子陈正敏遯斋闲览曽慥集仙传 - Refers to the inclusion of quotes or references from various works by different authors.
  • 方舟先生 - A title or nickname for Li Shi, meaning 'Mr. Boat.'
  • 绍兴乾道间人 - Refers to the period during which Li Shi lived, specifically the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 剽掇説部以为之 - Suggests that the text was compiled by gathering material from various sources.
  • 仍其旧文末及削改 - Indicates that the text was not significantly altered from its original form.

Proper Nouns

  • 钦定四库全书 - The *Siku Quanshu*.
  • 子部十二 - The 'Miscellaneous' or 'Novel' category within the *Siku Quanshu*.
  • 续博物志 - The title of the text.
  • 小説家三【琐记之属】 - The subcategory of 'Miscellaneous Notes' within the 'Novel' category.
  • 提要 - The summary or preface.
  • 谨按 - A formal expression.
  • 旧本题晋李石撰 - The original attribution to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.
  • 今上于前朝作镇睢阳 - The current emperor's previous station in Suizhou.
  • 开国号大宋 - The founding of the Song dynasty.
  • 全摭陆佃埤雅之説 - The reference to Lu Dian's *Piyaya*.
  • 引子华子陈正敏遯斋闲览曽慥集仙传 - References to other works.
  • 方舟先生 - A title or nickname for Li Shi.
  • 误也 - An indication of error.
  • 绍兴乾道间人 - The period during which Li Shi lived.
  • 剽掇説部以为之 - The method of compilation.
  • 仍其旧文末及削改 - The lack of significant alteration.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 惟华书首地理此首天象 - A comparative phrase indicating that the Chinese text begins with geography while the source text starts with astronomy, highlighting a difference in structure.
  • 体例小异 - Means 'the structure is slightly different.'
  • 次第仿华说 - Means 'the order follows the Chinese saying.'
  • 一事续一事 - Means 'one thing follows another.'
  • 龟巢莲叶一条 - A specific phrase that appears to be a reference to a particular section or story.
  • 复出竟不及检 - Means 'repeatedly appearing without being checked.'
  • 自相矛盾 - Means 'contradictory to itself.'
  • 附防舛误 - Means 'errors in connection or addition.'
  • 轶闻琐语 - Means 'anecdotes and trivial sayings.'
  • 姑录以备参考云尔 - Means 'record them for reference.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 华书 - Refers to the Chinese text or literature.
  • 王士祯 - A famous Qing dynasty poet and scholar.
  • 香祖笔记 - A collection of notes by Wang Shizhen.
  • 刘亮 - A historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 陈子真 - Another historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Han.
  • 欧阳生 - A historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 伏生 - A historical figure known for his contributions to the *Shujing* (Book of Documents).
  • 漯水 - A river in China.
  • 汲郡 - A historical region in China.
  • 宋人旧笈 - Refers to old collections from the Song dynasty.
  • 乾隆四十六年十月 - The date of the proofreading and submission, corresponding to October 1781 in the Gregorian calendar.

Proper Nouns

  • 华书 - The Chinese text or literature.
  • 王士祯 - Wang Shizhen, a Qing dynasty poet and scholar.
  • 香祖笔记 - Wang Shizhen's collection of notes.
  • 刘亮 - Liu Liang, a historical figure.
  • 陈子真 - Chen Zhen, a historical figure.
  • 文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han.
  • 欧阳生 - Ouyang Sheng, a historical figure.
  • 伏生 - Fu Sheng, a historical figure.
  • 漯水 - Luo River, a river in China.
  • 汲郡 - Jiyi, a historical region in China.
  • 宋人旧笈 - Old collections from the Song dynasty.
  • 乾隆四十六年十月 - October 1781 in the Gregorian calendar.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 總纂官 - Formal title meaning 'Chief Compiler,' used for the officials overseeing the compilation of the text.
  • 總校官 - Formal title meaning 'Chief Proofreader,' used for the official responsible for the final review.
  • 續博物志 - Title of the book, meaning 'Continuation of the *Museum of Wonders*,' a reference to the original work by Zhang Hua.
  • 原序 - Title of the preface, meaning 'Original Preface,' indicating that this is an introductory note.
  • 仿山海经 - Means 'imitating the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*,' referring to the style and content of Zhang Hua's original work.
  • 天官所遗 - Means 'what the *Celestial Officials* (a section of the *Book of Documents*) has left out,' referring to astronomical and calendrical knowledge.
  • 象纬之学 - Means 'the study of celestial phenomena,' referring to astronomy and astrology.
  • 从约见博 - Means 'to see breadth through conciseness,' a phrase emphasizing the value of concise yet comprehensive works.
  • 康熈戊申 - The date of the preface, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle (60-year cycle) to denote the year 1718.
  • 一阳月 - A term for the first month of the lunar year, often associated with the beginning of spring.
  • 星源汪士汉 - The name of the author of the preface, where '星源' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '汪士汉' is the personal name.
  • 钦定四库全书 - Title of the imperial collection, meaning 'Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*,' a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned during the Qing dynasty.
  • 李石撰 - Title of the author, meaning 'compiled by Li Shi,' indicating that Li Shi is the compiler of the *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*.
  • 张华述 - Title of the original author, meaning 'compiled by Zhang Hua,' indicating that Zhang Hua is the original compiler of the *Museum of Wonders*.
  • 地理 - Refers to geography, specifically the study of places and their characteristics.
  • 象纬 - Refers to celestial phenomena, specifically the study of the stars and their movements.
  • 山海经 - Title of the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing myths, legends, and geographical information.
  • 天官书 - Title of the *Celestial Officials* section of the *Book of Documents*, a classical Chinese text on astronomy and calendrical science.
  • 象纬之学 - The study of celestial phenomena, specifically the study of the stars and their movements.
  • 从约见博 - The phrase means 'to see breadth through conciseness,' emphasizing the value of concise yet comprehensive works.
  • 吴兴董氏 - The name of the person who expanded the work, where '吴兴' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '董氏' is the family name.
  • 博物者 - Refers to scholars or experts in the field of natural history and geography.
  • 考核 - Means 'to examine and verify,' referring to the process of careful research and verification.
  • 仍二篇之旧 - Means 'to retain the original two volumes,' indicating that the work is being presented in the same format as the original.
  • 各书十卷 - Means 'each volume consists of ten chapters,' indicating the structure of the work.
  • 便于采览 - Means 'convenient for reading and reference,' emphasizing the practicality of the work.
  • 不失博物之意 - Means 'does not lose the spirit of natural history,' indicating that the work maintains the original intent of the *Museum of Wonders*.
  • 吴兴董氏又广之而为五十卷 - Means 'Wu Xing Dong expanded it to fifty volumes,' indicating the expansion of the work.
  • 更为考核 - Means 'to conduct further examination and verification,' emphasizing the rigorous research process.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and writer of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his work *Museum of Wonders*.
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful emperor of the Han dynasty known for his military campaigns and cultural patronage.
  • 山海经 - The *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing myths, legends, and geographical information.
  • 天官书 - The *Celestial Officials* section of the *Book of Documents*, a classical Chinese text on astronomy and calendrical science.
  • 博物志 - The *Museum of Wonders*, a work by Zhang Hua that compiles various pieces of knowledge and curiosities.
  • 续博物志 - The *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*, a work that expands on Zhang Hua's original text.
  • 康熈戊申 - The year 1718, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle (60-year cycle) to denote the year.
  • 一阳月 - The first month of the lunar year, often associated with the beginning of spring.
  • 星源汪士汉 - The author of the preface, where '星源' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '汪士汉' is the personal name.
  • 钦定四库全书 - The *Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*, a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned during the Qing dynasty.
  • 李石 - Li Shi, the compiler of the *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*.
  • 吴兴董氏 - Wu Xing Dong, the person who expanded the work, where '吴兴' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '董氏' is the family name.

Proper Nouns

  • 总纂官 - Chief Compiler
  • 总校官 - Chief Proofreader
  • 张华 - Zhang Hua
  • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han
  • 山海经 - *Classic of Mountains and Seas*
  • 天官书 - *Celestial Officials*
  • 博物志 - *Museum of Wonders*
  • 续博物志 - *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*
  • 康熈戊申 - 1718
  • 一阳月 - First month of the lunar year
  • 星源汪士汉 - Author of the preface
  • 钦定四库全书 - *Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*
  • 李石 - Li Shi
  • 吴兴董氏 - Wu Xing Dong

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 爾雅既曰釋天不得不略言其趣 - Refers to the *Erya*, an ancient Chinese dictionary. 'Since it explains the heavens, it must briefly discuss the general principles or meanings.'
  • 凡有六等 - Means 'there are six categories' or 'there are six types.'
  • 形如弹丸 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'shaped like a ball' or 'shaped like a ball of clay.'
  • 天包其外 - Means 'the sky surrounds it on the outside.'
  • 犹如鸡卵白之绕黄 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like the white part of an egg surrounding the yolk.'
  • 虞氏所说 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
  • 穹隆在上 - Means 'arched above' or 'domed above.'

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 日有九道 - Classical expression referring to the nine paths or orbits of the sun, derived from ancient Chinese astronomical observations.
  • 考灵曜 - Refers to an ancient astronomical text, 'Kao Lingyao,' which discusses celestial phenomena.
  • 万世不失九道 - Statement indicating that the nine paths of the sun remain constant over time.
  • 郑注引河图帝览嬉 - Reference to Zheng Xuan's commentary on the 'River Chart' ('He Tu') and 'Emperor's Vision' ('Di Lanyi'), which are ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination.
  • 黄道一青道二出黄道东赤道二出黄道南白道二出黄道西黒道二出黄道北 - Describes the nine paths of the sun, with the central path (黄道) and eight additional paths (青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道) emanating from it in different directions.
  • 日春东从青道夏南从赤道秋西从白道冬北从黒道 - Explains the sun's movement along the nine paths during the four seasons.
  • 律厯志 - Refers to the 'Lüli Zhi' section of the 'Book of Han,' which discusses the calendar and astronomical observations.
  • 角一十二度亢九氐十五房五心五尾十八箕十一斗二十六牛八女十二虚十危十七营室十六壁九奎十六娄十二胃十四昴十一毕十六觜二参九井三十三四栁十五星七张十八翼十八轸十七 - Lists the degrees of the 28 lunar mansions (宿) in the Chinese zodiac, a traditional astronomical system.
  • 丑为星纪初斗十二度终于婺女七度子为枵初婺女八度终于危十五度亥为娵訾初危十六度终于奎四度戌为降娄初奎五度终于胃六度酉为大梁初胃七度终于毕十一度申为实沈初毕十二度终于井十五度未为鹑首初井十六度终于栁八度午为鹑火初栁九度终于张十七度已为鹑尾初张十八度终于轸十一度辰为夀星初轸十二度终于氐四度夘为大火初氐五度终于尾九度寅为析木初尾十度终于斗十一度 - Describes the divisions of the Chinese zodiac into 12 sections, each corresponding to a lunar mansion and a segment of the ecliptic.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 九道 - The concept of the nine paths of the sun is rooted in ancient Chinese astronomy, reflecting the belief in a structured and ordered universe.
  • 考灵曜 - An ancient text that discusses celestial phenomena, often used in conjunction with other astronomical works to understand the movements of celestial bodies.
  • 郑注 - Zheng Xuan, a prominent scholar of the Han dynasty, known for his commentaries on various classical texts.
  • 河图帝览嬉 - Ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination, often used to explain the structure of the universe and the movements of celestial bodies.
  • 律厯志 - A section of the 'Book of Han' that provides detailed information about the calendar and astronomical observations of the Han dynasty.
  • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions, a traditional system used in Chinese astronomy to divide the sky into 28 segments.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '日有九道故考灵曜云万世不失九道' introduces the concept of the nine paths of the sun and references an ancient text to support the idea that these paths are constant.
  • The complex sentence '郑注引河图帝览嬉云黄道一青道二出黄道东赤道二出黄道南白道二出黄道西黒道二出黄道北' describes the nine paths of the sun, with the central path (黄道) and eight additional paths (青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道) emanating from it in different directions.
  • The sentence '日春东从青道夏南从赤道秋西从白道冬北从黒道' explains the sun's movement along the nine paths during the four seasons.
  • The long list of degrees for the 28 lunar mansions must be read as a single, comprehensive catalogue.
  • The long list of zodiacal divisions must be read as a single, comprehensive catalogue, describing the segments of the ecliptic and their corresponding lunar mansions.

Proper Nouns

  • 考灵曜 - An ancient astronomical text.
  • 郑注 - Zheng Xuan, a prominent scholar of the Han dynasty.
  • 河图帝览嬉 - Ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination.
  • 律厯志 - A section of the 'Book of Han' discussing the calendar and astronomical observations.
  • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 漢律厯志 - Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar, a historical record of the calendar system during the Han Dynasty.
  • 上元泰初 - A term used in ancient Chinese astronomy and calendar calculations, referring to a specific epoch or starting point for astronomical observations.
  • 四千六百二十七嵗 - A numerical expression indicating a period of 4,627 years.
  • 元封七年 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, corresponding to 104 BCE.
  • 復得 - Means 'to encounter again' or 'to repeat,' referring to the recurrence of a particular celestial alignment.
  • 中冬 - Refers to the middle of winter, specifically the winter solstice.
  • 孟康曰 - Indicates a commentary or explanation by Meng Kang, a scholar from the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 言復得者 - A phrase used to introduce an explanation or clarification.
  • 嵗在甲曰 - Refers to the position of the Heavenly Stems (天干) in the Chinese calendar system, where '甲' (Jia) is the first stem.
  • 在寅曰 - Refers to the position of the Earthly Branches (地支) in the Chinese calendar system, where '寅' (Yin) is the third branch.
  • 正月得甲則曰 - Indicates that in the first month of the year, if the stem is '甲' (Jia), it is referred to as '畢陬' (Bi Zou).
  • 二月得乙則曰 - Indicates that in the second month of the year, if the stem is '乙' (Yi), it is referred to as '橘如' (Ju Ru).
  • 八分二十八宿為十二次 - Refers to the division of the 28 lunar mansions (二十八宿) into 12 segments (十二次) for astrological purposes.
  • 太嵗在四孟四季則嵗行二宿 - Refers to the movement of the Tai Sui through the four beginning months (四孟) and the four seasons (四季) and the corresponding movement of the stars.
  • 二八十六三四十二而行二十八宿 - A numerical expression indicating the movement of the stars through the 28 lunar mansions over a period of 12 years.
  • 歲星年行一次也 - Refers to the movement of Jupiter (歲星) through the 12 segments (十二次) once every year.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 漢律厯志 - The 'Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar' is a historical document that records the calendar system and astronomical observations during the Han Dynasty.
  • 上元泰初 - This term is used in ancient Chinese astronomy to denote a specific epoch or starting point for astronomical calculations.
  • 元封七年 - This year corresponds to 104 BCE and is a significant date in the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 孟康 - Meng Kang was a scholar from the Three Kingdoms period known for his commentaries on the 'Records of the Han Dynasty.'
  • 太嵗 - The Tai Sui is a celestial deity in Chinese astrology that represents the annual cycle and is associated with the movement of the stars.
  • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions (二十八宿) are a traditional division of the sky in Chinese astronomy, each associated with a specific constellation or region of the sky.
  • 十二次 - The 12 segments (十二次) are a division of the 28 lunar mansions used in Chinese astrology to track the movement of the stars and planets.

Proper Nouns

  • 漢律厯志 - The 'Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar.'
  • 上元泰初 - A specific epoch or starting point in ancient Chinese astronomy.
  • 元封七年 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, corresponding to 104 BCE.
  • 孟康 - A scholar from the Three Kingdoms period known for his commentaries on the 'Records of the Han Dynasty.'
  • 太嵗 - The Tai Sui, a celestial deity in Chinese astrology.
  • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions in Chinese astronomy.
  • 十二次 - The 12 segments used in Chinese astrology to track the movement of the stars and planets.
  • 歲星 - Jupiter, the planet associated with the annual cycle in Chinese astrology.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 山海东荒经曰 - Formal introduction indicating the following text is from the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), a classical Chinese text describing ancient geography and mythology.
  • 大言 - A term used in the *Shan Hai Jing* to describe a mountain where the sun and moon rise.
  • 波谷山 - A mountain name mentioned in the *Shan Hai Jing*, likely referring to a specific geographical feature.
  • 大人之国 - Refers to a mythical land of giants or a land inhabited by large beings.
  • 中容之国 - Refers to a mythical land of moderation or balance.
  • 二仪气分 - A phrase from the *Huainanzi*, meaning the division of the two principles (heaven and earth) into different forms.
  • 物理论云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Wu Lun* (Treatise on Things), a text discussing the nature of things.
  • 水土之气升为天 - A statement from the *Wu Lun*, meaning that the vapor from water and soil rises to form the sky.
  • 广雅云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Guangya* (Expanded Meaning), a dictionary of classical Chinese.
  • 五经通义云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Wu Jing Tongyi* (General Meaning of the Five Classics), a text explaining the meanings of the Five Classics.
  • 昊天上帝 - Refers to the supreme god in Chinese cosmology.
  • 五帝 - Refers to the five emperors in Chinese mythology.
  • 淮南子云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Huainanzi*, a philosophical text.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山海东荒经 - The *Shan Hai Jing* is an ancient Chinese text that describes the geography, mythology, and legends of ancient China.
  • 广雅 - The *Guangya* is a dictionary of classical Chinese, providing definitions and explanations of words and phrases.
  • 五经通义 - The *Wu Jing Tongyi* is a text that explains the meanings of the Five Classics, which are the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
  • 淮南子 - The *Huainanzi* is a philosophical text that discusses various topics, including cosmology, politics, and ethics.

Proper Nouns

  • 山海东荒经 - The *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
  • 广雅 - The *Guangya* (Expanded Meaning).
  • 五经通义 - The *Wu Jing Tongyi* (General Meaning of the Five Classics).
  • 淮南子 - The *Huainanzi* (Master Huainan).
  • 旸谷 - A mythical place where the sun rises.
  • 咸池 - A mythical place where the sun bathes.
  • 扶桑 - A mythical tree where the sun rests.
  • 曲阿 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 桑野 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 衡阳 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 昆吾 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 鸟次 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 悲谷 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 女纪 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 泉隅 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
  • 六螭 - A mythical creature that the sun rests on.
  • 虞泉 - A mythical place where the sun sets.
  • 蒙谷 - A mythical place where the sun sets.
  • 崦嵫 - A mythical place where the sun sets.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 白人之南建木之下日中无影 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'at the south of the White Man's land under the Great Tree where there is no shadow at noon,' referring to a mythical or symbolic location in ancient Chinese geography.
  • 天地之中也 - Means 'the center of the world' or 'the heart of heaven and earth.'
  • 月立夏夏至行南方赤道曰南陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the summer solstice, indicating it travels along the southern path of the celestial equator, known as the 'Southern Land.'
  • 立秋秋分行西方白道曰西陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the autumn equinox, indicating it travels along the western path of the Milky Way, known as the 'Western Land.'
  • 立冬冬至行北方曰北陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the winter solstice, indicating it travels along the northern path, known as the 'Northern Land.'
  • 晦而见西方谓之脁朔而见东方谓之朒亦谓之侧匿脁健行朒缩迟 - Complex description of the moon's phases, where '晦' (dark) and '朔' (new) refer to the new moon, and '朒' (dim) and '侧匿' (hidden) describe the waning moon. '脁健行' (strongly moving) and '朒缩迟' (slowly contracting) describe the moon's movements during different phases.
  • 郑司农云 - Indicates a citation from the scholar Zheng Sinao.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吕氏春秋 - A classic text compiled by the scholars of the state of Qin during the Warring States period, which covers various aspects of philosophy, politics, and science.
  • 汉书 - A historical text compiled by Ban Gu during the Eastern Han dynasty, which records the history of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 周官 - Also known as the *Rites of Zhou*, an ancient text that describes the organization of the Zhou dynasty's government and rituals.
  • 郑司农 - Zheng Sinao, a scholar from the Western Han dynasty known for his contributions to the study of divination and astronomy.
  • 保章氏 - An official responsible for astronomical observations and divination in ancient China.
  • 五云之物 - The five types of clouds observed during the solstices and equinoxes, each with its own significance.
  • 二至二分 - Refers to the solstices and equinoxes, which were important in ancient Chinese calendrical and astronomical systems.
  • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a court jester and scholar of the Han dynasty.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '白人之南建木之下日中无影盖天地之中也' is ambiguous and could be interpreted as 'At the south of the White Man's land under the Great Tree where there is no shadow at noon, it is said to be the center of the world.'
  • The long sentence '月立夏夏至行南方赤道曰南陆立秋秋分行西方白道曰西陆立冬冬至行北方曰北陆分则同道至则相过晦而见西方谓之脁朔而见东方谓之朒亦谓之侧匿脁健行朒缩迟' describes the moon's movements during the solstices and equinoxes, with each part detailing a specific phase and path.
  • The sentence '郑司农云保章氏五云之物二至二分观云色青虫白丧赤兵荒黑水黄丰东方朔别传云凡占东畔当视天有黄云来如覆车五谷大熟青致兵白致盗乌黑多水赤火' combines a citation from Zheng Sinao with a description of the interpretation of auspicious signs, which can be broken down into two main parts for clarity.

  • 至是果一千年因以一行为圣人 - Describes how the prophecy was fulfilled when the calendar was corrected after a thousand years, and Yi Xing was recognized as the sage.
  • 僧一行本名璲俗姓张氏后出家改名敬贤迁化诏起塔铜人原諡大恵禅师并御制碑八分书 - Describes the life of Yi Xing, originally named Sui, who later became a monk and was posthumously honored as a great master with a bronze statue and an imperial stele.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 二十八宿 - Refers to the 28 lunar mansions in Chinese astronomy, each associated with specific stars and constellations.
  • 赤道 - Refers to the celestial equator, the projection of Earth's equator onto the celestial sphere.
  • 黄道 - Refers to the ecliptic, the apparent path of the Sun across the sky.
  • 景纬 - Refers to the stars and constellations, often used in the context of astronomical phenomena.
  • 海田可变 - A metaphor suggesting that the sea and land can change, but the stars and constellations remain constant.
  • 中台告坼 - A reference to a star in the constellation of the Central Platform (中台) signaling a division or separation.
  • 月犯少微 - A term meaning 'the moon offends the minor star,' referring to a lunar conjunction with a specific star that was believed to have negative implications.
  • 蓬飞硙 - A phrase meaning 'the flying willow fluff,' used to describe a natural phenomenon.
  • 雷不盖酱 - A superstition that thunder should not cover the pot of soy sauce, as it is believed to cause internal thunder in the body.
  • 隔辙雨 - A term for a type of rain that occurs when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
  • 紫蜺矞云 - Refers to a colorful cloud, often seen as a sign of good fortune.
  • 君子小人并进 - A phrase meaning 'gentlemen and small people advance together,' suggesting a mixed or mixed-motives environment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 二十八星 - The 28 stars associated with the 28 lunar mansions, each with its own significance in Chinese astrology.
  • 刘知防 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official, known for his commentary on astronomy.
  • 九州万国 - A phrase referring to the nine regions and myriad states of ancient China, symbolizing the vastness of the empire.
  • 荧惑退舍 - A term referring to the planet Mars moving backward in its orbit, often associated with significant events.
  • 宋公延龄 - Refers to a historical event or person, possibly a ruler or noble who benefited from the alignment of Mars.
  • 晋相速祸 - Refers to a historical event or person, possibly a minister or official who faced misfortune due to celestial signs.
  • 太岁 - A term in Chinese astrology referring to the celestial stem that governs the year and influences human affairs.
  • 藕生应月 - A phrase meaning 'the lotus grows in response to the moon,' suggesting a natural phenomenon linked to the lunar cycle.
  • 十二子为卫 - A term referring to the 12 zodiac animals, often used in calendrical and astrological contexts.
  • 分龙雨 - A term for a type of rain that occurs when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
  • 蓬蠓 - A type of small insect or flying creature, often used in natural phenomena descriptions.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is complex and requires careful parsing. It states that the 28 lunar mansions were established because there are 28 stars in each mansion, and the degrees are divided accordingly. However, some stars do not fit neatly into these divisions, leading to variations in interpretation.
  • This sentence explains that the positions of stars are measured using the celestial equator (赤道), but the ecliptic (黄道) has varying angles, making it different from the celestial equator.
  • This sentence introduces a commentary by Liu Zhifang, who argues that the sun and stars are fixed in the sky, unlike the changing landscape of the earth, and that recording such constancy in historical texts is essential.
  • This sentence criticizes the *Records of the Grand Historian* (*Shiji*) for its inaccuracies and the *Book of Han* for including non-Han matters in its *Astronomical Records*.
  • This sentence discusses the inclusion of celestial phenomena in historical records, questioning why such phenomena are recorded while human affairs are not.
  • This sentence describes various superstitions and natural phenomena linked to the lunar cycle and the 12 zodiac animals.
  • This sentence explains a common belief about rain occurring when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
  • This sentence describes colorful clouds and their symbolic meanings, often seen as signs of good fortune or mixed motives.
  • This sentence includes a commentary by Guo Pu, describing natural phenomena and associated superstitions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 續博物志 - Title of the text, a continuation of the *博物志* (Wubaozhi), a classical text on natural history and miscellaneous knowledge.
  • 四庫全書 - A massive imperial collection of Chinese literature and texts, compiled during the Qing dynasty. The phrase '欽定' indicates that it was officially sanctioned by the emperor.
  • 漢明帝為太子 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Han when he was a crown prince. This provides historical context for the events described.
  • 樂人以歌四章賛之 - Musicians composed four songs to praise something. The term '賛之' implies a form of laudatory or celebratory expression.
  • 白蟻聞竹雞之聲化為水 - An unusual and fantastical description, where termites are said to turn into water when they hear the call of the bamboo partridge.
  • 方諸大蛤也 - A reference to the *方諸* (Fangzhu), a mythical stone that can collect dew when placed under the moon. '大蛤' means 'large clam' or 'large shellfish.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 漢明帝 - Emperor Ming of Han (r. 57–75 AD), the third emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty. He was known for his contributions to Buddhism and his efforts to strengthen the empire.
  • 四庫全書 - A monumental collection of Chinese literature, compiled in the late 18th century during the Qing dynasty. It includes a vast array of texts, from Confucian classics to historical records and literary works.
  • 續博物志 - A continuation of the *博物志* by Zhang Hua, a famous scholar of the Western Jin dynasty. The *博物志* is a compendium of natural history, mythology, and folklore.
  • 禽經 - An ancient text on birds, likely dating back to the Han dynasty. It contains descriptions of bird behavior and their symbolic meanings.
  • 高誘注淮南子 - A commentary by Gao You on the *Huainanzi*, a philosophical and encyclopedic text from the Han dynasty. The *Huainanzi* is a collection of essays on various topics, including cosmology, politics, and natural phenomena.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 日月蚀而私者生儿则多疾 - Superstitious belief that conceiving during a solar or lunar eclipse results in a child prone to illness.
  • 日月晦朔望而私者生儿则愚痴瘖痖 - Superstitious belief that conceiving during the new moon, full moon, or quarter moon results in a child with intellectual disabilities or speech impediments.
  • 钩绞丫戾逐阵而私者生儿多凶暴无礼 - Belief that conceiving during certain celestial alignments or patterns leads to a child with aggressive and unruly behavior.
  • 亦犹木日造麴而酸水日造酱则生虫九焦日种谷则不生芽六合日遣不去火日安蜂则苦土日种麻则不生 - Analogies comparing various activities performed on specific days to undesirable outcomes, such as spoiled food, pests, and failed crops.
  • 麈尾能留红扫毡毡不蠧 - Belief that using a dust broom (麈尾) can prevent redness on the carpet and keep it from rotting.
  • 李卫公言鵞惊防防猒火孔雀辟恶 - Statement attributed to Li Weigong that geese can scare away ghosts, and peacocks can ward off evil.
  • 水母生儿无目鼈生儿无耳 - Belief that jellyfish give birth to blind offspring, and turtles and softshell turtles give birth to deaf offspring.
  • 桐庐人尝伐竹遗竹见一宿竿雉头蛇身犹未变乃知竹为蛇蛇为雉头先化曽于青城山小路上见之 - Story about a person from Tonglu who, while cutting bamboo, saw a snake that had transformed into a pheasant's head and body, indicating that bamboo can turn into snakes and vice versa.
  • 所以夀者无死气于中也 - Belief that longevity is due to the absence of death energy within.
  • 河水东北有石室室中若有积卷因谓之积书岩每见神人往还世人不悟其为仙者又谓之神彼土目曰唐述因名其山曰唐述山堂曰唐述窟道术之士多往栖焉 - Description of a stone chamber in the northeast of the Yellow River, where scrolls are stored, and where immortals are said to visit. The locals call these spirits 'Tangshu,' and the mountain and cave are named accordingly.
  • 巫咸山在安县东南巫咸右手操青蛇左手操赤蛇羣巫所从上下大荒西经曰巫咸即巫朌巫彭巫姑巫眞巫孔巫抵巫谢巫罗 - Description of Wuxian Mountain in the southeast of An County, where the spirit Wuxian holds a green snake in his right hand and a red snake in his left, and where other spirits follow him. The *Classic of Mountains and Seas* mentions Wuxian as one of several spirits.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 日月蚀 - Refers to solar and lunar eclipses, which were often seen as omens in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 晦朔望 - Refers to the new moon, full moon, and quarter moon, which were significant in ancient Chinese astrology and superstitions.
  • 钩绞丫戾逐阵 - Refers to celestial patterns and alignments, which were believed to influence human affairs.
  • 麈尾 - A traditional Chinese dust broom made from the tail of a deer, used for sweeping and believed to have protective properties.
  • 李卫公 - Refers to Li Weigong, a historical figure known for his military and political achievements.
  • 鵞惊防防猒火孔雀辟恶 - References to geese, fire, and peacocks, which were believed to have protective or repelling properties.
  • 水母生儿无目鼈生儿无耳 - References to jellyfish, turtles, and softshell turtles, which were believed to have unusual reproductive characteristics.
  • 桐庐人 - Refers to people from Tonglu, a place in Zhejiang Province.
  • 青城山 - A famous mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
  • 河水 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in Chinese history.

  • 神人 - Refers to immortals or divine beings, who were believed to visit certain places.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 正月上辰 - The first day of the first lunar month, traditionally a time for rituals and celebrations.
  • 出水边盥濯食蓬饵以祓邪 - A ritual involving washing in water, eating *pom* (a type of cake), and performing purification rites to ward off evil.
  • 汉仪也 - Indicates that this is a Han Dynasty ritual or custom.
  • 亦曰阿雏乌 - Another name for the young crows.
  • 乌鸦反哺为慈乌 - Describes the behavior of crows feeding their parents, symbolizing filial piety.
  • 腹下白不反哺为鸦乌 - Describes a variant of the crow that does not feed its parents and has a white belly.
  • 白头羣飞为燕乌 - Describes crows with white heads that fly in groups.
  • 大而白头为仓乌 - Describes large crows with white heads.
  • 卑居为楚乌 - Describes crows that live in low-lying areas.
  • 又鸦乌为贾乌 - Another name for crows, possibly related to commerce or trade.
  • 庄子曰鸦贾也 - A reference to the philosopher Zhuangzi, who described crows as 'traders.'
  • 相家说人臣得龙之一体当至公相 - A statement from the *Xiang* (divination) tradition, suggesting that a minister who possesses a part of the dragon will achieve high office.
  • 曽公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛 - Specific examples of ministers who achieved high office, with 曾公亮 (Zeng Gongliang) having the dragon's spine and 王安石 (Wang Anshi) having the dragon's eye.
  • 鱼劳则尾赤人劳则髪白 - A proverb comparing the signs of hard work in fish (reddened tails) and humans (gray hair).
  • 鳣黄鱼口在颔下无鳞长鼻软骨俗谓玉板长二三丈江东呼为黄鱼 - Describes a type of fish, the *zhān* (a kind of yellow croaker), with specific physical characteristics and regional names.
  • 网鱼得鱮不如啖茹 - A proverb suggesting that catching fish is less rewarding than eating vegetables.
  • 南越志曰乌鲗懐墨而知礼江东人或取其墨书契以绐人物书迹如淡墨逾年墨消空纸耳 - A reference to the *Nanyue* (Southern Yue) records, describing the *wèi* (cuttlefish) and its use in writing and deception.
  • 抱朴子蟾蜍夀至千嵗者头上有角颔下有丹书八字 - A reference to the *Baopu Zi* (Master Who Embraces Simplicity), describing the longevity and characteristics of toads.
  • 裴頠崇有论曰鸟无胃肺蛤蜃无脏蛭以空中而生以无胃而育 - A reference to the *Chong You Lun* (Treatise on the Importance of Existence) by Pei Wei, discussing the unique biological features of birds and shellfish.
  • 聒抱者鸺鹠鹳鹊也影抱者鼈鼋也 - Describes animals that make noise when held (owls, storks, and crows) and those that cast shadows (turtles, tortoises, and softshell turtles).
  • 视防制柂观鱼制帆 - A metaphor comparing vigilance to steering a ship and observing fish to adjusting a sail.
  • 神和子姓屈突名无为字无不为张咏布衣时遇之海蟾子姓刘名昭逺华山陈抟馆之道院与种放往来广徳先生杜光庭处州人仕唐为内供奉避乱入蜀王氏爵为师臣 - Describes several historical figures, including their names, titles, and significant events in their lives.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伪蜀广政中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Guangzheng of the Later Shu state, a brief kingdom in Chinese history.
  • 饮酒食猪臓 - Literally 'drinking wine and eating pig intestines,' likely an idiom or euphemism for a specific ritual or practice.
  • 渝州刺史 - Refers to the governor of Yuzhou, an administrative region in ancient China.
  • 幻惑 - Means 'illusion' or 'deception,' often used to describe supernatural or mystical phenomena.
  • 竹笼沈之江 - Literally 'put in a bamboo cage and submerged in the river,' a method of execution or disposal.
  • 防为渔人所得 - Means 'was later found by a fisherman,' indicating a miraculous or unexpected outcome.
  • 上升严补阙 - Refers to ascending to a higher spiritual or official position, specifically the role of '补阙' (a type of official in the imperial court).
  • 青金丹方 - Refers to a formula for an elixir of immortality, often associated with alchemy.
  • 朱桃椎薛稷图其形 - Refers to the artists Zhu Taozhui and Xue Ji, who painted the figure of the subject.
  • 传于世 - Means 'passed down to future generations,' indicating the enduring legacy of the figure.
  • 吕洞賔谓矦生曰 - Refers to Lu Dongbin, a legendary Taoist immortal, speaking to someone named Hou Sheng.
  • 蛇蝎天地淘以自然之气 - Literally 'snakes and scorpions are refined by the natural qi of heaven and earth,' a metaphor for the refinement of natural elements.
  • 有损无益 - Means 'there is harm but no benefit,' a conclusion about the above statement.
  • 取纸语三口共一室 - Literally 'take a piece of paper, write three mouths in one room,' a riddle or metaphor.
  • 室畔水偏清 - Means 'the water beside the room is unusually clear,' a clue in the riddle.
  • 吕洞宾字 - Refers to Lu Dongbin's name or nickname, often associated with his legendary status.
  • 陈希夷先生 - Refers to Chen Xiyi, a legendary Taoist figure known for his longevity and spiritual practices.
  • 一睡或半嵗或三数月 - Means 'sleeping for half a year or several months,' a description of his unique sleeping habits.
  • 近亦不下月余 - Means 'recently not less than a month or more,' indicating a change in his sleeping pattern.
  • 留蔵真息 - Means 'preserving true breath,' a reference to Taoist practices of breath control.
  • 饮纳玉液 - Means 'drinking the jade liquid,' a metaphor for consuming elixirs of immortality.
  • 吾睡真睡也 - Means 'my sleep is true sleep,' a statement about the depth of his sleep.
  • 鲤鱼大小并三十六鳞 - Literally 'carp, large or small, all have thirty-six scales,' a description of carp.
  • 猬能跳入虎耳中 - Means 'the hedgehog can jump into the tiger's ear,' a metaphor for the hedgehog's agility.
  • 见鹊便自仰腹受啄 - Means 'when it sees a magpie, it lies on its belly and lets it peck,' a description of the hedgehog's behavior.
  • 水蛭亦如鱼子 - Means 'water leeches are also like fish eggs,' a comparison between the two.
  • 经三年得水犹活 - Means 'after three years, if they get water, they still live,' indicating the leeches' resilience.
  • 蛇蜕塞两耳 - Means 'snake skin is used to plug both ears,' a traditional remedy.
  • 治疟疾 - Means 'to treat malaria,' indicating the purpose of the remedy.
  • 覆盆子是苺子笮取汁 - Means 'goji berries are made from strawberry juice,' a description of a food preparation method.
  • 合成膏涂发不白 - Means 'mixed into a paste to prevent white hair,' indicating a cosmetic use.
  • 麞鹿非八卦辰属不荤羶 - Means 'musk deer and deer are not impure or stinky unless they belong to the eight trigrams and the twelve animals,' a statement about their purity.
  • 能温补于人生死无尤 - Means 'they can warm and supplement the body, with no blame in life or death,' indicating their medicinal value.
  • 道家听用为脯 - Means 'Taoists use them as dried meat,' indicating their use in Taoist practices.
  • 天宝中 - Refers to the Tianbao era, a period in the Tang dynasty.
  • 河南缑氏县太子陵仙观 - Refers to the Xianyao Temple in Taizi陵, a location in Henan province.
  • 每年九月三日夜 - Means 'every September 3rd night,' indicating a specific date.
  • 有道士一人得仙 - Means 'there is a Taoist who becomes an immortal,' indicating a miraculous event.
  • 已有旧例 - Means 'there is an old precedent,' indicating that this event is not new.
  • 至日具姓名申府 - Means 'on the day, report the name to the prefecture,' indicating a formal procedure.
  • 张竭忠为令不之信 - Means 'Zhang Jiezhong, the prefect, did not believe it,' indicating skepticism.
  • 阴令二勇士执兵觇之 - Means 'secretly ordered two brave men to carry weapons and spy on it,' indicating surveillance.
  • 至三更有一黒虎入观来衔一道士 - Means 'at three o'clock at night, a black tiger entered the temple and carried away a Taoist,' indicating a supernatural event.
  • 射之不中 - Means 'shot at it but missed,' indicating an unsuccessful attempt.
  • 弃道士而去 - Means 'left the Taoist and went away,' indicating the tiger's departure.

  • 水蛭亦如鱼子 - Means 'water leeches are also like fish eggs,' a comparison between the two.
  • 经三年得水犹活 - Means 'after three years, if they get water, they still live,' indicating the leeches' resilience.
  • 蛇蜕塞两耳 - Means 'snake skin is used to plug both ears,' a traditional remedy.
  • 治疟疾 - Means 'to treat malaria,' indicating the purpose of the remedy.
  • 覆盆子是苺子笮取汁 - Means 'goji berries are made from strawberry juice,' a description of a food preparation method.
  • 合成膏涂发不白 - Means 'mixed into a paste to prevent white hair,' indicating a cosmetic use.
  • 麞鹿非八卦辰属不荤羶 - Means 'musk deer and deer are not impure or stinky unless they belong to the eight trigrams and the twelve animals,' a statement about their purity.
  • 能温补于人生死无尤 - Means 'they can warm and supplement the body, with no blame in life or death,' indicating their medicinal value.
  • 道家听用为脯 - Means 'Taoists use them as dried meat,' indicating their use in Taoist practices.
  • 天宝中 - Refers to the Tianbao era, a period in the Tang dynasty.
  • 河南缑氏县太子陵仙观 - Refers to the Xianyao Temple in Taizi陵, a location in Henan province.
  • 每年九月三日夜 - Means 'every September 3rd night,' indicating a specific date.
  • 有道士一人得仙 - Means 'there is a Taoist who becomes an immortal,' indicating a miraculous event.
  • 已有旧例 - Means 'there is an old precedent,' indicating that this event is not new.
  • 至日具姓名申府 - Means 'on the day, report the name to the prefecture,' indicating a formal procedure.
  • 张竭忠为令不之信 - Means 'Zhang Jiezhong, the prefect, did not believe it,' indicating skepticism.
  • 阴令二勇士执兵觇之 - Means 'secretly ordered two brave men to carry weapons and spy on it,' indicating surveillance.
  • 至三更有一黒虎入观来衔一道士 - Means 'at three o'clock at night, a black tiger entered the temple and carried away a Taoist,' indicating a supernatural event.
  • 射之不中 - Means 'shot at it but missed,' indicating an unsuccessful attempt.
  • 弃道士而去 - Means 'left the Taoist and went away,' indicating the tiger's departure.
  • 令于是申府请弓矢 - Means 'the prefect then reported to the prefecture and requested bows and arrows,' indicating a response.
  • 大猎石穴中格杀数虎 - Means 'a large hunt in the stone cave killed several tigers,' indicating a successful hunt.

  • 嫁之有子 - Means 'she was married and had children.'
  • 太和中有开周世冡得殉葬女子数日而有气数日而能言 - Means 'during the reign of Emperor Wu of Wei (226-239 AD), when a Zhou dynasty tomb was opened, a woman who had been buried as a sacrifice revived after several days and could speak after more days.'
  • 郭太后爱养之 - Means 'Empress Guo took care of her.'

  • 南方木凝其大如槩鼻涕闻人气则闪闪而动堕人体成疮以麝香朱砂涂之愈 - Means 'in the south, there is a type of tree that hardens; its size is like a large pestle. When it comes into contact with human breath, it moves slightly and can cause sores on the body. It can be treated with musk and cinnabar.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汉武故事 - A collection of stories about Emperor Wu of Han, a famous ruler of the Han Dynasty.
  • 东郡送一短人长七寸曰巨灵 - A literal description of a short person from Dong Commandery, seven inches tall, named Juling.
  • 光武时颍川张仲师长二寸 - Zhang Zhongshi from Yingchuan during the Guangwu era, who was two inches tall.
  • 东方朔曰西北荒中有小人长七寸朱衣冠国男女皆长七寸海鹄吞之腹中不死 - Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar, about a mythical land in the northwest where small people seven inches tall wear red clothes and black hats, and when swallowed by a sea bird, they do not die.
  • 子华子曰黄帝之治天下也其精微之感荡上沈而下浮为百福之聚是百神受职于庚也 - Zihua Zi, a sage, about Huangdi's governance of the world, where the subtle influence of his spirit moves up and down, gathering a hundred blessings, and all spirits receive their duties from him.
  • 帝乃采铜炼刚质也登彼山就髙明也作大罏者鼓阳化神鼎者熟物之器也上水而下火二气升降以相济中和之气也 - Huangdi collecting copper and refining it into a solid substance, ascending Mount Taimu to seek enlightenment, making a large furnace, and using it to transform into a divine tripod, which is a vessel for cooking, with water on top and fire below, representing the interaction of the two forces to achieve harmony.
  • 羣龙者众阳之气云者龙属也帝乡者灵台之阙而心术之变也 - The collective energy of the dragons, which are associated with the clouds, and the imperial residence, which is the gateway to the spiritual realm and the transformation of the mind.
  • 弓裘衣冠者帝之所以善世救俗之具也 - The bow, robe, hat, and crown, which are the tools Huangdi used to improve the world and save customs.
  • 甚矣世之好怪也后世必有人主好高而慕大以久生轻举为羡慕者左右狡诈希宠之臣又从而逢之是将甘心于黄帝之所造也 - How the world loves strange things, and in the future, there will be rulers who admire Huangdi's height and grandeur, and seek immortality, and their cunning and deceitful ministers will follow suit, desiring to achieve what Huangdi did.
  • 玉局观洞高骈帅蜀取罪人以绳绊其腰令探浅深绳两月方絶出青城山洞天观门 - Yuju Cave, where Gao Pian, the governor of Shu, tied criminals to ropes to explore the depth, and after two months, they emerged from the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven.
  • 巫山道士黄万防以符药救人蜀王建召之铺草席水面泝流而上一日至成都省郎曹图鄙其人 - Huang Wanhfang, a Daoist priest from Wushan, who used talismans and medicine to save people, and was summoned by Wang Jian, the king of Shu, who laid out a mat on the water and floated upstream to Chengdu, where the provincial censor Cao Tu looked down on him.

  • 月氏国使者献香 - Indicates that an envoy from the Yuezhi Kingdom presented the incense.
  • 东风入律百旬不休 - Describes a prolonged period of favorable winds, symbolizing auspicious conditions.
  • 青云干吕连月不散 - Refers to a continuous period of clear skies and auspicious signs.
  • 捜竒蕴异 - Means to search for and contain rare and extraordinary things.

  • 帝分香烧之 - Means the emperor distributed and burned the incense.
  • 死未三日皆活 - Indicates that those who had died within three days came back to life.
  • 芳气三月不歇 - Describes the fragrance lasting for three months.
  • 余香一旦失亡 - Means the remaining fragrance suddenly disappeared.
  • 凉土异物志 - Refers to a record of strange things in the Liang region.
  • 葱岭水分流东西 - Describes the flow of water from Mount Kongling (Kunlun) splitting into eastern and western directions.
  • 西入大海 - Indicates that the water flows west into the sea.
  • 东为河源 - Means the eastern flow becomes the source of the Yellow River.

  • 潜发于蒲昌 - Indicates that the river emerges from the Puchang area.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 积石一千七百四十里 - Geographical measurement indicating the distance of the mountain range, which is 1,740 li.
  • 西海之南流沙之滨 - Describes the location as being south of the Western Sea and along the banks of flowing sand.
  • 赤水之后黒水之前 - Indicates a position between the Red Water and the Black Water.
  • 有大山曰昆仑 - Introduces the name of a large mountain, Kunlun.
  • 又曰钟山西六百里 - Another name for the mountain, located 600 li west of Zhongshan.
  • 周穆王昆仑瑶池 - Refers to the legendary King Mu of Zhou and the mythical Pool of Jade at Kunlun.
  • 去周之瀍涧特万有一千一百里 - Indicates the distance from the Zhou capital, specifically the Cai and Jian rivers, which is 11,100 li.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蘐草一名鹿葱花名宜男 - 蘐草是鹿葱的别名，宜男暗示与男性后代有关，可能指一种被认为能促进男性生育的植物。
  • 風土記云姙婦佩其花生男也 - 風土記是一本关于地方风俗和地理的书籍，姙婦佩其花生男也指怀孕的妇女佩戴这种花会生男孩，这是一种民间信仰。
  • 李衛公云金錢花損眼 - 李衛公是历史人物，金錢花損眼指金钱花对眼睛有害，这是一种关于植物效果的信仰。
  • 牡丹初不載文字惟以藥見本草唐則天以後洛花始盛 - 牡丹最初只在药书中提到，唐则天之后洛阳的牡丹开始繁荣。
  • 沈宋元白亦不及也 - 沈宋元白指诗人沈佺期、宋之问、元稹和白居易，他们也没有提到牡丹。
  • 劉夢得有咏魚朝恩宅牡丹但云一丛千朶謝靈運言永嘉竹間多牡丹今越花不及洛花逺甚 - 劉夢得写了关于鱼朝恩宅邸牡丹的诗，但只说一丛千朵；谢灵运说永嘉竹林间有很多牡丹，现在越地的牡丹远不如洛阳的。
  • 或曰靈運之所謂牡丹今之芍藥特盛於吳越 - 有人认为谢灵运所说的牡丹实际上是芍药，在吴越地区特别繁盛。
  • 杞菊山芋牛蒡道家以為嘉蔬江淮百合根大而味甘南地微苦其次決明合歡槐芽亦可食孫思邈以合歡為萱草嵇叔夜合歡蠲忿萱草忘憂兩物也 - 杞菊山芋牛蒡是多种植物，道家认为它们是佳蔬；江淮地区的百合根大而味甜，南方的稍苦；决明、合欢、槐芽也可以食用；孙思邈认为合欢是萱草，嵇叔夜认为合欢和萱草可以缓解愤怒和忘记忧虑。
  • 山蕭一名山缲神異經作永嘉郡記作魅一名山骆一名蛟一名濯肉一名肉一名晖一名飛龍如鸠青色亦曰治鳥巢大如五斗器飾以土垩赤白相間狀如射侯犯者役虎害人燒人廬舍昔值洪水食都樹皮饿死化為鳥姚汪王三姓其孫也 - 山蕭是山缲的别名，神异经称其为某种东西，永嘉郡记称其为魅；它还有其他名字，如山骆、蛟、濯肉、肉、晖、飞龙等，也被称为治鸟，巢穴大如五斗器，装饰以红白土，形状像箭靶；冒犯它的人会被老虎奴役，受到伤害，房子被烧；过去在洪水期间，人们吃都城的树皮，饿死变成鸟，姚、汪、王三姓的后代。
  • 俗諱五月上屋言五月人蜕上屋見影魂當去 - 五月上屋顶是禁忌，据说五月人蜕皮，上屋顶看到自己的影子意味着灵魂会离开。
  • 二廣有草名胡蔓以急水吞之即死慢水即緩死取毒蛇殺之以此草覆之洒水菌生其上末為毒藥殺人 - 两广地区有一种草叫胡蔓，用急水吞服会立即死亡，用慢水吞服会慢慢死亡；取毒蛇杀死后，用这种草覆盖，洒水后长出的菌子可以制成毒药杀人。

'至用之不勤' suggests using this energy without effort. '此章全是黄帝之言' implies the chapter is entirely attributed to the Yellow Emperor.   • 骠国诸蛮并不养娑罗木子破其殻中如栁絮细织为幅服之谓之娑罗笼段 - The people of Biao Kingdom do not cultivate the seeds of the Sera tree; when broken open, they contain fine fibers like willow fluff, which are woven into fabric called Sera Longduan (describing the cultivation and use of the Sera tree).
  • 蛮法煮盐成圑为颗质则以颗计之 - The barbarian method of cooking salt into lumps, each lump counted as a unit (describing the method of salting and counting).
  • 茶出银生诸山采无时杂椒姜烹而饮之 - Tea comes from the silver-producing mountains, harvested at any time, mixed with pepper and ginger, and brewed for drinking (describing the cultivation and preparation of tea).
  • 生金出长傍诸山取法以春或冬先于山腹掘坑方夏水潦荡沙泥土注之坑秋始披而拣之有得片块大者重一斤或二斤小者不下三四两先纳官十分之八余许归私仍累劳効免征赋麸金出丽水河睒川有罪送淘金所为重役防同川银山出银矿私置冶官十之三诺睒川有锡山出锡 - Native gold is found in the mountains near Chang, harvested in spring or winter by digging pits in the mountain, filled with water and silt in summer, and then sifted in autumn. Large pieces weigh one or two catties, smaller ones at least three or four taels. Eight-tenths go to the government, the rest to the private sector, with accumulated labor exempt from taxes. Flourish gold is found in Lishui River and Heishan Creek; those with crimes are sent to the gold washing place for heavy labor. Silver is mined in the mountains of Fangtong River, with private smelting offices taking three-tenths. Tin is found in the mountains of Nuo River (describing the mining and distribution of gold, silver, and tin).
  • 苏躭郴县人尝与众儿共牧牛更直为师録每至躭直牛不逐自还既而辞母仙去又说年疫穿一井饮之可得无恙 - Su Dan, a native of Chencounty, once herded cows with other children, taking turns to be the leader. Whenever it was his turn, the cows would return on their own. Later, he left his mother to become a immortal. He also said that during an epidemic, digging a well and drinking from it could prevent illness (describing the life and magical abilities of Su Dan).
  • 道士谭紫霄有观灯之术说人祸福必中 - Taoist Tan Zixiao has the skill of observing lights, predicting people's fortune and misfortune, always accurately (describing the magical abilities of Taoist Tan Zixiao).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 賀元仕石晉為水部員外郎 - He Yuan served as an external officer of the Water Department during the Later Jin dynasty.
  • 天聖初使弟子喻澄詣闕獻浮屠老子像直數十萬 - During the early Tian Sheng period, he sent his disciple Yu Cheng to the imperial court to present statues of Buddha and Laozi, valued at tens of thousands of coins.
  • 毛女在華山山客獵師世世見之 - Describes a woman with fur on her body, seen by hunters and their teachers on Mount Hua generation after generation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 好道而不知求道之方 - Means 'loves the Dao but does not know the way to seek it,' highlighting a common theme in Daoist literature of loving the Dao without understanding its path.
  • 惟朝夕拜跪向一枯树 - Means 'only worships and kneels to a dry tree all day and night,' indicating a specific ritualistic practice.
  • 輙云乞长生 - Means 'always says to beg for immortality,' emphasizing the repetitive nature of the prayer.
  • 如此二十八年不倦 - Means 'like this for twenty-eight years without tiring,' highlighting the persistence of the practice.
  • 枯木一旦忽然生华 - Means 'the dry tree suddenly blossoms,' indicating an unexpected and miraculous event.
  • 华又有汁甜如 - Means 'the flowers have sap as sweet as honey,' describing the miraculous quality of the sap.
  • 遂取此华及汁并食之 - Means 'then he took the flowers and sap and ate them together,' indicating the consumption of the miraculous substance.
  • 食讫即仙 - Means 'after eating, he became an immortal,' indicating the immediate result of the miraculous consumption.
  • 刘少翁曽数入太华山礼拜向山如此二十年 - Means 'Liu Shao-weng often entered Taihuashan to worship the mountain like this for twenty years,' highlighting his persistent practice.
  • 遂忽一旦得见西岳丈人授其仙道 - Means 'he suddenly met the Western Peak Elder and was taught the path to immortality,' indicating the immediate result of his persistent practice.
  • 一人数旦旦诣河边拜河水如此十年 - Means 'one person went to the river every day to worship the river water like this for ten years,' highlighting the persistent practice.
  • 河侯河伯遂与相见与之白璧十双教授水行不溺法 - Means 'the River Lord and River God then met him, gave him ten pairs of white jade, and taught him the method of walking on water without drowning,' indicating the immediate result of his persistent practice.
  • 薏苡一名簳珠子蒸令气馏曝干挼取之作饭麪主不饥 - Means 'Coix seed, also known as bamboo pearl, is steamed until steam distills, dried in the sun, and then made into rice or noodles to prevent hunger,' indicating a medicinal use.
  • 作干姜法水淹三日毕置流水中六日更去皮然后曝干入瓮瓶谓之醸也 - Means 'The method of making dried ginger involves soaking it in water for three days, then placing it in running water for six days, removing the skin, and then drying it in the sun before placing it in jars or bottles, which is called 'fermentation,' indicating a specific preparation method.
  • 淫羊藿一名仙灵脾淫羊一日百遍食藿所致 - Means 'Epimedium, also known as fairy spleen, is caused by a goat eating it a hundred times a day,' indicating a medicinal plant with a specific origin story.
  • 新生艾作干菜妙作馄饨吞三五枚以饭压之良疗一切鬼气 - Means 'Newly harvested mugwort is made into dried vegetables, which are excellent for making dumplings. Eating three or five dumplings with rice can effectively treat all kinds of ghostly illnesses,' indicating a medicinal use.
  • 苏合香本草云生中台川陶云是师子屎又云是诸香汁煎之非一物从西域来者如紫檀重如石蘡薁是山葡萄 - Means 'Storax, according to the *Bencao*, is said to grow in the Central Plains. Tao Hongjing said it is lion dung, and others say it is a mixture of various fragrant juices. It is not a single substance but comes from the Western Regions, like sandalwood, heavy as stone, and is also known as mountain grapes,' indicating a complex description of the source and properties of storax.

  • 山石皆然 - The stones in the mountain all burn, suggesting a widespread and intense phenomenon.
  • 炎起數丈 - Flames rise several zhang (a unit of length), indicating a significant height and intensity.
  • 大黒風 - A strong, dark wind, often associated with storms or natural disasters.
  • 掩之 - Covering or blocking, suggesting an attempt to extinguish or control the fire.
  • 其石如初 - The stones return to their original state, indicating a cyclical or self-renewing process.
  • 不敢輕近耳 - One dares not approach lightly, suggesting a sense of awe or fear.
  • 火井 - A well that produces fire, likely a reference to natural gas or oil, indicating a source of energy.
  • 草爨之 - Using grass to ignite, a common method for starting fires.
  • 煙騰火發 - Smoke rises and fire ignites, indicating a sudden and dramatic event.
  • 湯井 - A well that produces hot water, likely a reference to a natural hot spring, indicating a source of heat.
  • 草納之 - Using grass to introduce or activate, a common method for starting or maintaining fires.
  • 露凝 - Dew condenses, indicating a cool or humid environment.
  • 鐸銷 - A bell melts, suggesting a high temperature or intense heat.
  • 狀如刀戈殘刄 - Shaped like the remnants of swords and spears, indicating a sharp or jagged appearance.
  • 有孔旁達 - With holes extending, suggesting a network or interconnected system.
  • 出麗水 - Emerging from a beautiful river, indicating a source of beauty or purity.
  • 飾以金 - Decorated with gold, suggesting a precious or valuable object.
  • 所指無不洞夷 - Pointing at anything makes it penetrate or smooth, suggesting a powerful or transformative effect.
  • 人尤寳 - People especially treasure it, indicating a high value or importance.
  • 月以血祭之 - Sacrificing blood to it under the moon, suggesting a ritual or ceremonial practice.
  • 俗謂天降 - Commonly said to be a heavenly descent, indicating a divine or supernatural origin.
  • 非人铸 - Not made by humans, suggesting a natural or supernatural origin.
  • 郁刃 - A type of blade or sword, possibly a reference to a specific type of weapon.
  • 铸以毒药 - Casting with poison, suggesting a dangerous or harmful process.
  • 冶取跃如星者 - Smelting and selecting those that leap like stars, indicating a selective and precise process.
  • 淬以马血 - Quenching with horse blood, suggesting a traditional or ritualistic process.
  • 伤人即死 - Harming someone results in death, indicating a deadly or lethal effect.
  • 刀劒 - Swords and knives, indicating weapons or tools.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天寳中 - Refers to the Tianbao era (742-756) of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
  • 交趾 - Refers to Jiaozhi, an ancient Chinese term for the region that is now northern Vietnam.
  • 贡龙脑 - ‘贡’ means to present as a tribute, and ‘龙脑’ refers to camphor or borneol, a fragrant resin used in medicine and incense.
  • 如蝉 - Describes the appearance of the camphor, comparing it to cicada wings.
  • 老龙脑树节方有 - Refers to the rare occurrence of camphor in the bark of old camphor trees.
  • 禁中 - Refers to the inner palace or the emperor's private quarters.
  • 瑞龙脑 - ‘瑞’ means auspicious, so ‘瑞龙脑’ is a special, auspicious form of camphor.
  • 上惟赐贵妃十枚 - ‘上’ refers to the emperor, and ‘贵妃’ refers to Yang Guifei, Emperor Xuanzong's favorite concubine.
  • 香气彻十余歩 - Describes the strong fragrance of the camphor that could be smelled from a considerable distance.
  • 蓬莱山 - Refers to Mount Penglai, a legendary mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and the divine.
  • 使高丽者 - Refers to envoys sent to Goryeo (Korea).
  • 望之甚逺 - Means that the mountain could be seen from a great distance.
  • 前后下峭拔可爱 - Describes the mountain's steep and majestic appearance.
  • 岛属昌国县 - Indicates that the island belongs to Changguo County.
  • 其上平广可以种莳 - Describes the island's flat and expansive surface suitable for cultivation.
  • 马绪谪潮得巨蚁长尺余 - ‘谪’ means exile, and ‘潮’ refers to the coastal region. The sentence describes an ant found by Ma Xu, who was exiled to the coast, that was unusually large.
  • 盐渍之 - Means the ant was salted for preservation.
  • 归夸北人 - Refers to Ma Xu returning to the north and boasting about the ant to others.
  • 两头蛇 - Refers to a snake with two heads, a rare and unusual creature.
  • 马鼈食牛血所化 - Suggests that the two-headed snake is a result of a horse turtle (a type of turtle) eating cow blood.
  • 鸤鸠 - Refers to the cuckoo bird.
  • 秸鞠 - Refers to a type of bird, possibly a sparrow or another small bird.
  • 布谷 - Another name for the cuckoo bird.
  • 雎鸠 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the mandarin duck in Chinese literature.
  • 王睢 - Another name for the mandarin duck.
  • 卑居 - Refers to a type of bird, possibly a sparrow or another small bird.
  • 鸦乌 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with crows or ravens.
  • 小雀好羣飞 - Describes a small bird that likes to fly in flocks.
  • 桑扈 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the cuckoo.
  • 窃脂 - Refers to a bird that steals fat, possibly a reference to a specific bird known for this behavior.
  • 青雀 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the blue magpie.
  • 海州有人持一束黑物形如竹篾 - Describes a person from Hai Prefecture holding a bundle of black material shaped like bamboo strips.
  • 海鱼腮中毛 - Refers to the hair-like substance found in the gills of a sea fish.
  • 可作屛风贴 - Means the substance can be used as a material for screen decorations.
  • 色似水牛角头似猪鬃 - Describes the color and texture of the substance, comparing it to water buffalo horn and pig bristles.
  • 长三四寸广可一寸 - Specifies the dimensions of the substance.
  • 人家有小虫至微而响甚 - Describes a tiny insect that makes a loud sound.
  • 寻之卒不可见 - Means the insect is difficult to find despite its loud noise.
  • 号窃虫 - Refers to the insect by the name 'qie chong'.
  • 大如半胡麻 - Describes the size of the insect, comparing it to half a sesame seed.
  • 形似鼠妇有两角白色 - Describes the shape and appearance of the insect, comparing it to a woodlouse and noting its white color with two horns.
  • 振其头则有声 - Means shaking the insect's head produces a sound.
  • 孟康朝作赋比之鬼魅 - Refers to Meng Kang, a scholar who wrote a poem comparing the insect to a ghost or demon.
  • 海州有沟水通淮过漕船 - Describes a canal in Hai Prefecture that connects to the Huai River and is used for transporting grain.
  • 寳应中堰破水涸 - Refers to the Baoying period (762-763) when a dam broke, causing the water to dry up.
  • 东海主修堰 - Indicates that the Eastern Sea (likely referring to the East China Sea) was responsible for repairing the dam.
  • 堰成輙壊 - Means the dam would repeatedly break after being repaired.
  • 或说梁代筑浮山堰以铁数万斤填积其下乃成 - Suggests that during the Liang Dynasty, a dam was built at Fushan using thousands of pounds of iron to stabilize it.
  • 堰之壊如雷声至是声闻上流 - Describes the dam breaking with a sound like thunder, which could be heard upstream.
  • 盖铁味辛辛能害目鱼龙防目故畏铁也 - Explains that the dam broke because the iron had a pungent taste that harmed the eyes of fish and dragons, causing them to avoid the area.
  • 有人淬劒于池池鱼皆浮去 - Describes someone tempering a sword in a pond, causing the fish to float away due to the disturbance.

  • 贡龙脑 - Camphor was a valuable commodity in ancient China, used for medicinal and aromatic purposes.

  • 贵妃 - Yang Guifei was one of the most famous concubines in Chinese history, known for her beauty and influence over Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 蓬莱山 - Mount Penglai is a mythical mountain in Chinese folklore, often associated with immortals and the divine.
  • 使高丽者 - Envoys to Goryeo (Korea) were important for diplomatic and trade relations.
  • 马绪 - Ma Xu was a historical figure, though the specific details of his exile are not well-documented.
  • 两头蛇 - Two-headed snakes are rare and often considered omens in Chinese culture.
  • 马鼈 - A type of turtle that is not commonly known in modern Chinese.
  • 鸤鸠 - The cuckoo bird is often associated with spring and renewal in Chinese poetry.
  • 秸鞠 - A type of bird that is not well-documented in historical texts.

  • 雎鸠 - The mandarin duck is a symbol of love and fidelity in Chinese culture.

  • 卑居 - A type of bird that is not well-documented in historical texts.
  • 鸦乌 - Crows and ravens are often associated with death and the supernatural in Chinese folklore.
  • 小雀好羣飞 - Small birds flying in flocks are a common sight in nature and are often used in poetry to describe peaceful scenes.
  • 桑扈 - The cuckoo bird is often associated with the cuckoo in Chinese literature.
  • 窃脂 - A bird that steals fat is a rare and unusual creature, often used in folklore.
  • 青雀 - The blue magpie is a colorful bird that is often associated with good luck.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 接食圑饭 - Literal expression meaning 'catching and eating round rice,' likely referring to a bird's feeding behavior.
  • 投之高下无失 - Means 'throwing it high or low without missing,' describing the bird's accuracy in catching food.
  • 巫山迎船鸟 - Refers to a bird associated with the Wushan area, known for greeting ships.
  • 凡鸟三指向前一向后 - Describes the typical bird's wing structure with three forward-facing fingers and one backward-facing finger.
  • 鹦鹉两指向后 - Specifies that parrots have two fingers pointing backward.
  • 汉明帝永平五年 - Indicates the year 62 AD during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han.
  • 长安迎取飞廉并铜马 - Refers to the act of bringing the wind god Feilian and a bronze horse from Chang'an.
  • 置上西门外 - Means 'placed at the west gate of the upper city.'
  • 晋灼曰 - Refers to Jin Zhuo, a commentator, who provides an explanation.
  • 飞廉鹿身头如雀有角而蛇尾豹文 - Describes Feilian as having a deer body, a bird's head, antlers, a snake's tail, and leopard-like patterns.
  • 应劭曰 - Refers to Ying Shao, another commentator, who provides an alternative explanation.
  • 飞廉神禽能致风气 - Describes Feilian as a divine bird capable of summoning wind.
  • 宣王好射三石之弓 - Refers to King Xuan of Zhou, who enjoyed shooting a bow that could draw three stones.
  • 自以为九石 - Means 'considered it to be nine stones,' indicating a misperception of its strength.
  • 臣下从而悦之 - Means 'ministers followed suit and flattered him,' suggesting a culture of flattery.
  • 以非大王不能用其实三石也九石名而已 - Explains that the actual strength was three stones, but it was called nine stones, indicating a discrepancy between reality and perception.
  • 周官全羽为旞析羽为旌 - Refers to the Zhou ritual text, where full feathers are used for *suì* and split feathers for *jīng*.
  • 道路用旌节即此旌也 - Indicates that the *jīng* used on roads is the same as the *jīng* described.
  • 顾恺之画苏武所执之旌 - Refers to Gu Kaizhi's painting of Su Wu holding a *jīng*.
  • 上圆如幢下复数层红羽骖骖然如夜合花 - Describes the *jīng* as having a round top like a banner, multiple layers of red feathers, and a shape resembling a night-blooming flower.
  • 此析羽也 - Indicates that this is an example of split feathers.
  • 今卤簿中节颇此 - Refers to the current imperial processions, where the *jié* (staff) is similar to the *jīng*.
  • 其首亦圆相去尺余有数重圆板以牦牛尾缀之 - Describes the *jié* as having a round top, spaced about a foot apart, with several layers of circular plates attached by yak tails.
  • 亦析羽遗法 - Indicates that this is a legacy of the split feather tradition.
  • 苏武汉节则旌节之制 - Refers to Su Wu's *jié*, which follows the *jīng* and *jié* system.
  • 至于龙节虎节玉节之 - Refers to different types of *jié* used by various ranks, including dragon, tiger, and jade *jié*.
  • 则今虎符之 - Indicates that these are similar to the current tiger-shaped seals.
  • 但可合符不必尽为旌耳 - Suggests that these *jié* are used for verification but not necessarily as *jīng*.
  • 顾画节首一物若书卷 - Describes the *jié* as having a top that looks like a scroll.
  • 岂亦符乎 - Questions whether this is also a seal.
  • 汉铜符以铜为之 - Refers to the Han dynasty's copper seals.
  • 竹符以竹五寸镌刻篆书其上 - Describes the Han dynasty's bamboo seals, which were five inches long and inscribed with seal script.
  • 砚谱载天下之砚四十余品 - Refers to a catalog of over forty types of inkstones.
  • 以青州红丝石砚为第一 - Indicates that the red silk stone inkstone from Qingzhou is ranked first.
  • 端州斧柯山石为第二 - Indicates that the stone from Fuxi Mountain in Duanzhou is ranked second.
  • 歙州龙尾石为第三 - Indicates that the dragon-tail stone from Shezhou is ranked third.
  • 夜蔵饮食于噐中覆之不宻 - Refers to storing food in a container that is not tightly covered at night.
  • 鼠欲盗食不可至环噐而走 - Describes mice trying to steal food but being unable to reach it, running around the container.
  • 泪滴噐中食之得黄疾通身如蜡 - Indicates that the mice's tears fall into the food, causing a disease that makes their bodies turn yellow and waxy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鍮石金 - A comparison where *鍮石* (bronze-like stone) is likened to *金* (gold), suggesting a false or inferior counterpart.
  • 碔砆玉 - A comparison where *碔砆* (a kind of stone) is likened to *玉* (jade), again indicating a false or inferior counterpart.
  • 木兰类桂 - Magnolia is compared to cinnamon for its fragrance and value.
  • 奚醢类檀 - A type of sauce is compared to sandalwood for its aroma.
  • 蛇牀类蘼芜 - Herbs are compared for their medicinal properties.
  • 人参类荠苨 - Ginseng is compared to other plants for its medicinal value.
  • 桧类柏 - Cypress is compared to pine for its appearance and use.
  • 狐狸狗 - Foxes and dogs are compared for their behavior and appearance.
  • 鸑鷟鳯 - Mythical birds are compared for their beauty and significance.
  • 野马麟 - Wild horses are compared to unicorns for their rarity and majesty.
  • 乘千里马往案之 - Refers to riding a thousand-mile horse to investigate, emphasizing speed and urgency.
  • 髪拳紫色状如八骏 - Describes the horse's appearance as curly-haired, purple, and resembling the eight legendary horses.
  • 乘者必衣裘冒絮似北人 - Suggests that only those who ride this horse wear furs and head coverings, implying a northern origin.
  • 抱持早发扶风 - Indicates that the rider held the horse tightly and set out early from Fufeng, emphasizing preparation and urgency.
  • 初驰三十里则皆旁侧跳 - Describes the horse's initial speed and jumping behavior, suggesting its exceptional qualities.
  • 三十里外乃隤然而去 - Indicates that after thirty miles, the horse slows down, showing its endurance and control.
  • 惟闻耳傍风声飞前蹄之土过耳 - Describes the sound of the horse's hooves and the sensation of being carried by the wind, emphasizing its speed and power.
  • 辰时达保定申时归奏事 - Indicates the precise timing of the journey, from 7 AM to 5 PM, highlighting the efficiency of the ride.
  • 颛帝有三子而亡去为疫鬼 - States that Emperor Zhuangdi had three sons who became plague demons, indicating a mythological background.
  • 其一居江水为疫鬼 - Suggests that one son resides in the Yangtze River and causes plagues, emphasizing the geographical distribution of the demons.
  • 其一居弱水为罔两 - Indicates that another son resides in the weak water and becomes a shadow demon, suggesting a different role.
  • 其一居人宫室塸堣善惊人小儿 - States that the third son resides in human dwellings and frightens children, emphasizing the specific behavior of the demon.
  • 脚弱病用杉木为桶濯足排樟脑两股间以脚掤系定月余即效 - Describes a treatment for foot weakness using a bucket made of cypress wood, camphor, and other materials, emphasizing the method and its effectiveness.
  • 杂令曰蹄人者绊其足齧人者截其耳 - Refers to a law that punishes those who kick or bite others by binding their feet or cutting off their ears, indicating strict legal measures.
  • 说文牛触者角着横木 - Refers to a dictionary entry that describes how to handle a bull that charges by placing a horizontal beam on its horns, emphasizing a practical solution.
  • 有一狠子生平多逆父防父临死嘱曰必葬我水中意其逆命得葬土中至是狠子曰生平逆父命死不敢达防也破家筑沙潭水心以葬 - Describes a son who often defied his father, who, on his deathbed, requested to be buried in water. The son, fearing to disobey, built a sand pond in the middle of a river to fulfill the request, emphasizing the son's obedience and the father's wishes.
  • 本草经曰虎啸风生龙吟云起磁石引鍼琥珀拾芥漆得蟹而散麻得漆而涌桂得葱而软树得桂而枯戎盐累獭胆分杯其气爽之相关感也 - Refers to the *Bencao Jing* (Herbal Classic), describing various natural phenomena and their interactions, emphasizing the interconnectedness of nature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一曰偏記二曰小録三曰逸事四曰鏁言五曰郡事六曰家史七曰别傳八曰雜記九曰地理十曰都邑 - A list of ten types of historical or biographical literature in classical Chinese. The terms are classical categories of historical writing, often used in official or private historiography.
  • 偏記 - Biased records or partial accounts (literally, 'partial records').
  • 小録 - Small records or minor annals.
  • 逸事 - Anecdotes or extraordinary events.
  • 鏁言 - Fragments or isolated sayings.
  • 郡事 - Affairs of the commanderies or local matters.
  • 家史 - Family history.
  • 别傳 - Separate biography or individual biography.
  • 雜記 - Miscellaneous records.
  • 地理 - Geography.
  • 都邑 - Capital cities or urban centers.
  • 如孕覺物漸下入股至足大拇痛甚拆而生珠如彈圓漸長大如杯 - A description of a bizarre birth, where the woman feels something descending into her leg, reaching the foot, and causing pain. She then 'splits' it open and gives birth to a 'pearl-like' object that grows to the size of a cup.
  • 如孕 - As if pregnant or in a state of pregnancy.
  • 覺物漸下入股至足 - Feels an object gradually descending into the thigh to the foot.
  • 大拇痛甚 - The big toe is very painful.
  • 拆而生珠如彈圓 - She splits it open and gives birth to a pearl-like object as round as a ball.
  • 漸長大如杯 - It gradually grows as large as a cup.
  • 京有小索亭世語以為本索氏兄弟居又為索水晉志京有大索小索亭漢書京索之間也 - A historical and geographical reference.
  • 京有小索亭 - The capital has a small Suo pavilion.
  • 世語以為本索氏兄弟居 - The *Shìyǔ* (a historical text) says that originally the brothers of the Suo family lived there.
  • 又為索水 - Also called the Suo River.
  • 晉志京有大索小索亭 - The *Jin Zhi* (a historical record of the Jin dynasty) says the capital has a large and small Suo pavilion.
  • 漢書京索之間也 - The *Book of Han* refers to the area between Jing and Suo.
  • 計然云人受命於天地變化而生一月而膏二月而脈三月而胚謂如水泡之狀四月而胎謂如水中虾防胎也五月而筋六月而骨謂血氣變為肉肉為脂脂為骨也七月而成形八月而動九月而躁十月而生 - A description of fetal development according to the classical text *Ji Ran*.
  • 計然 - A classical Chinese scholar and economist, known for his theories on natural cycles and economics.
  • 人受命於天地變化而生 - Human beings receive their mandate from the changes of heaven and earth and are born.
  • 一月而膏 - In the first month, the body is formed with fat.
  • 二月而脈 - In the second month, the pulse forms.
  • 三月而胚 - In the third month, the embryo is formed.
  • 謂如水泡之狀 - It is said to be like the shape of a water bubble.
  • 四月而胎 - In the fourth month, the fetus is formed.
  • 謂如水中虾防胎也 - It is said to resemble the shape of a shrimp in water.
  • 五月而筋 - In the fifth month, the tendons form.
  • 六月而骨 - In the sixth month, the bones form.
  • 謂血氣變為肉肉為脂脂為骨也 - It is said that blood and qi transform into flesh, flesh into fat, and fat into bone.
  • 七月而成形 - In the seventh month, the form is completed.
  • 八月而動 - In the eighth month, movement begins.
  • 九月而躁 - In the ninth month, the fetus becomes restless.
  • 十月而生 - In the tenth month, the child is born.
  • 方田 - Rectangular fields (land measurement).
  • 粟米 - Grain and rice (proportional exchange).
  • 差分 - Differences and proportions.
  • 少廣 - Small breadth (square root calculations).
  • 均輸 - Equal transport (distribution of labor or goods).
  • 方程 - Equations (linear equations).
  • 傍要 - Side essentials (geometric problems).
  • 盈足 - Excess and deficiency (methods for solving equations).
  • 語林云王中郎以圍棊為坐或亦謂之為手談又謂之為棊聖 - A reference to the classical text *Yu Lin* (a collection of anecdotes and sayings).
  • 圍棊 - Go or weiqi, the ancient Chinese board game.
  • 坐 - Seated conversation or game played while sitting.
  • 手談 - Hand conversation, a poetic term for playing Go.
  • 棊聖 - Master of Go or champion of the board.
  • 窭薮王叡云東方朔以為寄生賣餅人結茅為绖以戴頭上狀如環 - A reference to the *Ju Sǒu* (a collection of anecdotes), attributed to Wang Rui.
  • 寄生賣餅人 - A person who lives off selling cakes or a person who lives by selling food.
  • 結茅為绖 - Ties茅草 to make a绖, where *绖* is a mourning band or a kind of head covering.
  • 以戴頭上狀如環 - Wears it on the head, shaped like a ring.
  • 蟹鬭精上有孔其中有子有泥食之殺人 - A description of a dangerous creature, possibly a type of crab.
  • 蟹鬭精 - A term possibly referring to a type of crab or a specific part of it.
  • 上有孔 - It has a hole on top.
  • 其中有子有泥 - Inside it are eggs and mud.
  • 食之殺人 - Eating it is fatal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐大順元年六月資州兵王全義妻如孕覺物漸下入股至足大拇痛甚拆而生珠如彈圓漸長大如杯 - A passage that appears to be a record of an unusual birth or a medical anomaly, possibly from a historical or medical text.
  • 唐大順元年 - The first year of the Dashiun era of the Tang dynasty (890 CE).
  • 資州 - A state or region in ancient China, located in modern-day Sichuan.
  • 王全義 - A military officer or general from the Tang dynasty.
  • 西京相璠曰京有小索亭世語以為本索氏兄弟居又為索水晉志京有大索小索亭漢書京索之間也 - A historical and geographical reference.
  • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the Western Capital of the Han dynasty.
  • 相璠 - A historian or geographer, likely from the Jin dynasty.
  • 世語 - *Shiyu*, a historical text by Liu Zhiji, which contains anecdotes and records of the Han dynasty.
  • 晉志 - *Jin Zhi*, a historical record from the Jin dynasty.
